metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation AC item (or extend the docs/concurrency.md AC item) to require that the downgrade operator guidance explicitly names which consumer surfaces (show, list, search, export) lose creation_channel visibility and prescribes a notification/coordination step before the downgrade is executed.
evidence: 'Notify downstream show/list/export consumers before any full downgrade' is referenced in the plan body but does not appear in any AC doc target.
docs/concurrency.md AC item covers only the three-state schema matrix and compaction pause — not the consumer notification procedure.
No AC item names the consumer list or where the notification obligation is documented.
findings.finding: The plan references notifying 'downstream show/list/export consumers' before any full downgrade but assigns this guidance to no documentation artifact and includes no AC item to verify the notification procedure or consumer list is documented anywhere.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — 'Notify downstream show/list/export consumers before any full downgrade and document this matrix plus the unconditional compaction pause in operator guidance.'
scenarios: Operator follows docs/concurrency.md for the compaction pause but is unaware of the consumer-notification requirement; downstream integrations break silently after downgrade.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in 6fe2 (owner of _lib_writes.py) that _lib_writes.create_ticket gains an internal-only creation_channel kwarg (defaulting to 'python') that is forwarded to create_core, enabling e622's import_ndjson.py call to pass creation_channel='import' through the same public path.
evidence: e622 What: 'Have src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py invoke the shared library creation path with the internal import channel.'
import_ndjson.py line 229: local = create_ticket(repo_root=rr, **_provenance.create_kwargs(rec)) — calls the PUBLIC rebar.create_ticket with no channel arg
rebar._lib_writes.create_ticket signature: no creation_channel parameter exists
e622 file_impact does NOT list src/rebar/_lib_writes.py
6fe2 file_impact lists _lib_writes.py only for 'Python defaults and internal facade threading'
Parent plan: 'Every live producer declares its channel at its entry boundary. The shared core carries and validates the declared internal value'
container pairing: parent + 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/e622-a596-3ceb-4f74
findings.finding: The mechanism for threading creation_channel='import' from import_ndjson.py through the public rebar.create_ticket() API to create_core is unspecified and may be undeliverable: _lib_writes.create_ticket does not expose creation_channel, e622 does not list _lib_writes.py in its file_impact, and 6fe2 (which owns _lib_writes.py) only describes adding a 'python' default — leaving no defined owner for making the public wrapper accept an internal channel override for the import path.
findings.location: child e622-a596-3ceb-4f74 (Scope/What section: import channel threading) and child 6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41 (Files: _lib_writes.py)
scenarios: e622 ships without updating _lib_writes.create_ticket; import_ndjson.py cannot pass creation_channel='import'; every imported ticket gets creation_channel=python (the 6fe2 default) rather than import — AC4 fails.

e622 bypasses _lib_writes and calls composer.create_core directly; this works but is an undocumented bypass of the public API that breaks the library/CLI/MCP convergence guarantee and is not stated in either child's What/Files sections.
findings.suggested_fix: In 6fe2: add creation_channel: str = 'python' as an internal (underscore-prefixed or documented-internal) keyword parameter to _lib_writes.create_ticket; forward it to composer.create_core. In e622: update import_ndjson.py (or _provenance.create_kwargs) to pass creation_channel='import' through this parameter. Document in 6fe2's scope that _lib_writes wrappers get the internal forwarding kwarg.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add _lib_writes.py to e622's file_impact (or clarify in 6fe2) and confirm that _lib_writes.ensure_identity_for accepts and forwards creation_channel kwarg so the full Jira placeholder identity chain is unambiguously owned.
evidence: e622 What: '_ensure_inbound_assignee_identity passes creation_channel="jira" through rebar.ensure_identity_for -> identity.ensure_identity_for -> create_identity_core -> create_core'
apply_inbound_records.py line 69: rebar.ensure_identity_for('jira', account_id, ...) — calls the PUBLIC wrapper in _lib_writes.py
_lib_writes.ensure_identity_for current signature: (provider, external_id, display_name, *, repo_root=None) — no creation_channel

6fe2 What: 'Add creation_channel="python" keyword parameters to create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for' — ambiguous whether 'ensure_identity_for' refers to both the commands/identity.py version AND the _lib_writes.py wrapper
e622 file_impact: _lib_writes.py is ABSENT
container pairing: parent + 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/e622-a596-3ceb-4f74
findings.finding: The call chain e622 specifies for Jira placeholder identity channel attribution — _ensure_inbound_assignee_identity → rebar.ensure_identity_for → identity.ensure_identity_for → create_identity_core → create_core — requires the _lib_writes.py public wrapper rebar.ensure_identity_for to accept and forward creation_channel='jira', but e622 does not list _lib_writes.py in its file_impact and 6fe2's description of the signature change is limited to 'create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for' without explicitly naming the _lib_writes.py wrapper.
findings.location: child e622-a596-3ceb-4f74 (What section: placeholder identity chain) and child 6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41 (Files: _lib_writes.py, _commands/identity.py)
scenarios: 6fe2 adds creation_channel to identity.ensure_identity_for but not to _lib_writes.ensure_identity_for; e622 cannot pass creation_channel='jira' through the public wrapper; inbound placeholder identities get creation_channel=python instead of jira — e622 AC1 partially fails.
The gap is discovered at e622 integration time when apply_inbound_records calls rebar.ensure_identity_for with a creation_channel kwarg that the public wrapper rejects with TypeError.
findings.suggested_fix: In 6fe2: explicitly update _lib_writes.ensure_identity_for to accept creation_channel='python' and forward it to identity.ensure_identity_for. Document this in 6fe2's What/Files. In e622: add _lib_writes.py to file_impact and note that the Jira inbound call site passes creation_channel='jira' through the updated public wrapper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a restorative fallback to the downgrade procedure: specify a pre-downgrade checkpoint step (e.g., verify no SNAPSHOT exists for affected tickets, or snapshot the raw event log) so that if compaction runs on the old binary, affected tickets can be identified and recovery is possible (e.g., re-run reducer on retained raw events after re-upgrading).
evidence: 'The only binary rollback is a full downgrade to the immediately pre-feature binary, which hides the field and has an explicit first-compaction DATA LOSS RISK.'
'Before deploying the old binary, operators must stop every scheduled or manual rebar compact invocation and keep compaction/event retirement stopped for the entire downgrade window'
No event-log backup, compaction-dryrun gate, or restore-from-raw-events procedure is specified for the case where the compaction pause is violated.
T4 checklist: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.'
findings.finding: The rollback plan is purely preventive — it tells operators to stop `rebar compact` before downgrading — but provides no restorative path if an operator runs compaction on the old binary anyway. The T4 criterion requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not just a preventive warning; once the bad compaction occurs, creation_channel is silently and permanently lost from affected tickets with no described recovery.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — 'Compaction preserves creation_channel' paragraph and Risks and Mitigations — 'Snapshot data loss' bullet; AC item for docs/concurrency.md
scenarios: Operator overlooks the guidance and runs `rebar compact` on the old binary; all newly compacted tickets permanently lose creation_channel with no recovery.
Automated compaction job (cron/CI) is not paused; partial compaction occurs silently before the operator notices.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the downgrade section to require: (1) before switching binaries, record the set of ticket IDs that have been compacted (SNAPSHOT present) under the new binary so they are known to carry creation_channel; (2) if old-binary compaction is detected post-hoc (e.g., via a SNAPSHOT missing creation_channel), define recovery as: upgrade to new binary, run `rebar rebuild-snapshot` (or equivalent) on the affected ticket IDs to regenerate creation_channel from the raw CREATE event. Add this to docs/concurrency.md and gate it with a test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document whether `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` (which reduces from `.retired` files via `include_retired=True`) can recover `creation_channel` from a SNAPSHOT produced by an old binary during a violated downgrade window, and either add a compensating-forward recovery procedure to `docs/concurrency.md` or explicitly state the data is irrecoverable to justify the unconditional compaction-pause requirement.
evidence: Plan: 'a first old-code compaction of raw creation-bearing CREATE events would produce a SNAPSHOT without creation_channel, which is why compaction is prohibited during the window'
Plan explicitly labels this 'DATA LOSS RISK' but provides only the preventive operator precondition, not a recovery path after violation
docs/concurrency.md confirms .retired files are kept permanently: '.retired files are kept permanently for now — an accepted storage tradeoff that guarantees a folded source is never lost'
compact.py rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log already reduces from include_retired=True, meaning .retired CREATEs carrying creation_channel are still accessible after old-binary compaction
ABSENCE: the plan does not state whether fsck --repair-snapshots / rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log can recover the field from .retired CREATEs after a constraint violation
findings.finding: The plan identifies a DATA LOSS RISK if compaction runs during a binary downgrade window, but does not specify a compensating-forward recovery path for the case where that constraint is violated. Since .retired files are retained permanently (I1/docs/concurrency.md), rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log — which reduces from the full log including .retired files — could potentially recover the creation_channel value from a stranded .retired CREATE. The plan does not affirm or deny this recovery path, leaving operators without a documented procedure if the precondition is accidentally violated.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions section — 'The only binary rollback is a full downgrade…' paragraph; also child ticket 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87 description — 'Document the rollback schema-state matrix'; AC line: 'docs/concurrency.md defines the full-downgrade three-state schema matrix'
scenarios: A scheduled rebar compact-all job fires during the downgrade window before operators can stop it. The old binary compacts a CREATE carrying creation_channel into a SNAPSHOT that omits it. The plan says this is data loss, but operators have no documented recovery procedure.
A CI pipeline or a cron job triggers a single rebar compact during the downgrade window. The operators follow the rollback guide but miss stopping one automated compact invocation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph to the rollback guidance in `docs/concurrency.md` (and/or the plan's downgrade matrix): 'If compaction ran during the downgrade window, run `rebar fsck --repair-snapshots` after re-deploying the new binary — `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log` reduces from `.retired` files (which are retained permanently per I1) and will re-derive the `creation_channel` value that the old-binary SNAPSHOT omitted, restoring the field from the still-extant `.retired` CREATE event.' If this recovery does NOT work (e.g., because the old binary also retired the CREATE and the HLC ordering prevents correct re-reduction), document that explicitly and add a verification step to the downgrade checklist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #6's proof command to include at least one test file (e.g., `tests/interfaces/facades/test_creation_channel_output.py`) that exercises `rebar show`, `rebar list`, and `rebar search` command output and asserts the six-value enum field is present and correct in their results.
evidence: "Prove with uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_ticket_state.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py tests/interfaces/io/test_export_ndjson.py && python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check"
No test file targeting `show`, `list`, or `search` runtime output is cited; the tests named are schema/contract/export tests only.

The Success Criteria section separately states 'Auditors can distinguish cli, mcp, python, jira, and import creation through existing show/list/search state projections', reinforcing that runtime projection coverage is intended.
findings.finding: AC #6 claims show/list/search projections expose the six-value enum, but the cited proof command covers only schema-contract tests and type-generation checks — no test exercises the actual runtime show, list, or search command output to confirm the field appears there.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #6: 'TicketState schemas, generated types, show/list/search projections, plus exported NDJSON expose the six-value enum…'
scenarios: A schema correctly defines the enum but the reducer projection omits the field from the show/list payload; the AC would pass on the schema test while the runtime behavior silently fails.
The type-generation check passes but the show command's output serializer strips unknown keys before rendering.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `-k show or list or search` target inside `tests/interfaces/facades/test_creation_channel.py` or a dedicated contract test, then append it to AC #6's proof command alongside the existing schema tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC (or a documented manual validation step with a script) that exercises the rollback three-state matrix — e.g., a test that serializes a new-code SNAPSHOT and verifies an old-binary-equivalent reader (simulated via schema stripping logic) handles it as specified for each of the three cases.
evidence: The plan describes three states: (a) old binary + new SNAPSHOT retains creation_channel; (b) old binary first compaction of raw CREATE events loses the field; (c) never-compacted ticket retains the value in CREATE but old reader hides it.
AC7 and AC8 test compaction and SNAPSHOT-only replay with the NEW binary — neither AC exercises the old-binary behavior.
No AC invokes the pre-feature binary against post-feature data to verify the three-state matrix holds.
The AC for docs/concurrency.md (AC12) proves the documentation exists but not that the behavior is correct.
findings.finding: The rollback three-state schema matrix is documented in prose but no AC tests the actual downgrade path — specifically that an old binary reading new-code SNAPSHOTs, new CREATE events, or never-compacted tickets behaves as specified.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — rollback matrix; Acceptance Criteria — compaction ACs
scenarios: If operators initiate a rollback and the old binary behaves differently from what the three-state matrix predicts (e.g., process_snapshot does NOT restore unknown compiled_state keys as claimed), auditors will get silently wrong data without any test catching this discrepancy.

The claim that 'the old strip policy removes only updated_at plus signature' is a code assumption that could be invalidated by an unrelated patch; without a cross-binary behavioral test, this assumption is unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit Architecture Decision) specifying that the reducer ignores/rejects `creation_channel` on any non-CREATE event type, and prove it with a test that sends a mutation event containing `creation_channel` and asserts the compiled_state value is unchanged.
evidence: Title states 'immutable ticket creation channel'.

'Jira outbound bind/sync events preserve the already-reduced creation_channel and never supply a replacement value' — this is a behavioral statement about one call site, not a reducer-level invariant.
No AC tests that a UPDATE/PATCH/BIND event containing a creation_channel field is rejected or ignored by the reducer.
No AC tests that a second CREATE event for the same ticket cannot change creation_channel.
findings.finding: The plan claims `creation_channel` is 'immutable' in the title and Architecture Decisions, but provides no enforcement mechanism or test that rejects a subsequent event attempting to overwrite the field on an existing ticket.
findings.location: Title: 'Track immutable ticket creation channel'; Architecture Decisions — 'Add creation_channel to CREATE data and compiled ticket state'
scenarios: A future ingress or buggy writer emits an UPDATE event with a creation_channel field; without reducer enforcement, the compiled_state could silently mutate, breaking the immutability guarantee the title promises.
A Jira outbound sync event that inadvertently includes creation_channel in its payload would overwrite the recorded value.
findings.suggested_fix: In the reducer's compiled_state projection logic, treat creation_channel as a CREATE-only field: set it once from the first CREATE event and ignore it on all subsequent events. Add a corresponding test: `pytest -q tests/unit/reducer/test_creation_channel_immutability.py -k 'mutation_cannot_overwrite'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In 568c's tests for rebuild (test_creation_channel_compaction.py -k rebuild), assert the rebuilt SNAPSHOT's compiled_state contains the expected creation_channel and creation_channel_inferred values, not just that the ticket's status is correct.
evidence: compact.py lines 522-524: 'check = reduce_ticket(ticket_dir) / if check is None or check.get("status") != compiled_state.get("status"): / return False'
The round-trip assertion checks ONLY status, not creation_channel or creation_channel_inferred

568c AC5: 'rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log and conservative-horizon compaction preserve the same provenance while later live events replay normally. Prove with uv run pytest ... -k "rebuild or horizon"'

The test must cover this, but the production round-trip assertion in rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log does NOT verify creation_channel — a test could pass while the production safeguard misses a creation_channel mismatch
container pairing: parent + 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/e622-a596-3ceb-4f74
findings.finding: Child 568c's scope OUT states 'changing source_event_uuids semantics' but the rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log path in compact.py re-derives source_uuids from ALL raw events including retired ones; the round-trip check (line 523-524) only verifies status equality, not creation_channel equality — leaving a subtle gap where a rebuild could produce the correct status but wrong (or missing) creation_channel without failing the round-trip assertion.
findings.location: child 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87 (Acceptance Criteria AC5: rebuild or horizon) and src/rebar/_commands/compact.py lines 522-524
scenarios: A bug in 568c's post-snapshot legacy projection causes creation_channel to be wrong after rebuild; the status-only round-trip check passes; the bug ships silently.
An operator using fsck --repair-snapshots gets correct status but wrong creation_channel in rebuilt SNAPSHOTs.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_creation_channel_compaction.py (rebuild k-filter), after calling rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log, reduce the ticket and assert state['creation_channel'] == expected_channel and state.get('creation_channel_inferred') == expected_inferred.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the isolated spec-load contract test file (e.g., `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_inbound_translate_imports.py`) and add it to the relevant AC's proof command so its coverage is explicit and verifiable.
evidence: "pinned by an isolated spec-load contract test" — the plan asserts the test exists and is required but gives no file path, module, or test suite anchor.
All other tests in the plan are cited by exact file path (e.g., tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_creation_channel.py, tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_ticket_state.py).
findings.finding: The plan requires 'an isolated spec-load contract test' to pin the absolute-import pattern for LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR/LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID in inbound_translate, but neither names the test file nor states where it lives, leaving the executor to decide its location without a stated default.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — 'inbound_translate._event_meta performs the absolute package import … pinned by an isolated spec-load contract test'
scenarios: An executor uncertain whether to place the test in tests/unit/, tests/interfaces/contracts/, or a new module may choose inconsistently or omit it entirely.
The absence of a named file means no AC can reference it as proof, so the spec-load constraint is asserted in prose but never verified by a cited command.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to Architecture Decisions: 'The spec-load test lives at tests/interfaces/contracts/test_inbound_translate_imports.py and is included in the AC #5 or AC #2 proof command.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with a prove command referencing the spec-load contract test file) that verifies the _version.py import of LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR/LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID is isolated from the sibling reconciler package context.
evidence: "This does not depend on sibling reconciler package context and is pinned by an isolated spec-load contract test."
No AC references a test file or command that exercises this import-isolation contract.
findings.finding: The Architecture section explicitly describes an 'isolated spec-load contract test' that pins the _version.py import path independence from the sibling reconciler package context, but no acceptance criterion covers this test or its file path — leaving a described verification artifact without a covering criterion.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions (paragraph on LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR/LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID constants and inbound_translate import pattern)
scenarios: An implementer writes the import but omits or misplaces the contract test; no AC fails, so the gap goes undetected in CI.
A future refactor moves the constants out of _version.py; without an AC-backed test, the breakage is invisible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR and LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID are importable from rebar.reducer._version without the sibling reconciler package installed or on sys.path. Prove with uv run pytest -q tests/unit/reducer/test_version_constants_isolation.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the key architectural decisions: immutable creation-channel field, closed vocabulary rationale, Jira-only historical inference predicate (three-signal design and false-positive controls), additive-only contract, and the compaction-pause downgrade protocol — so this rationale survives ticket archival.
evidence: Plan mandates docs/event-schema.md, docs/output-schemas.md, docs/concurrency.md updates via AC lines — these are schema reference and operational guides, not architectural decision records.

The Architecture Decisions section contains rich rationale (inference predicate design, downgrade matrix, additive contract, compaction constraints) but it lives only in the ticket, not in a discoverable ADR or CLAUDE.md.
No AC line references an ADR file or CLAUDE.md entry.
'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design doc update' — criterion T5e(c).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (immutable creation-channel audit dimension with historical Jira inference and a compaction-pause downgrade protocol) but captures the architectural rationale only inside the ticket body, not in a durable ADR or CLAUDE.md update that future maintainers can discover independently of the ticket tracker.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions section; Scope/Risks; AC lines for docs/event-schema.md and docs/concurrency.md
scenarios: A future engineer adding a new ingress channel (e.g., GitHub reconciler) cannot easily find the rationale for the closed vocabulary or the inference predicate constraints without searching ticket history.
A maintainer evaluating whether to add a writer-only binary mode needs the documented rationale for why it was excluded from the MVP to avoid re-litigating the decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/creation-channel-provenance.md (or add a ## Creation Channel Provenance section to CLAUDE.md) summarising: (1) why creation_channel is immutable and additive, (2) the closed six-value vocabulary and its extension policy, (3) the three-signal Jira inference predicate and why only jira is inferred, (4) the downgrade compaction-pause requirement and the three-state compiled_state matrix. Reference this file from the existing docs/event-schema.md. Add a corresponding AC line: 'docs/adr/creation-channel-provenance.md exists and is linked from docs/event-schema.md. Prove with ls docs/adr/creation-channel-provenance.md && grep -q creation-channel-provenance docs/event-schema.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight deploy-time mechanism (e.g., an env-var flag `REBAR_CREATION_CHANNEL_ENABLED=1`) that gates new CREATE field writes so operators can enable the feature for a canary population before full rollout, or document why the additive-only nature of the change makes a 100%-flip safe enough to explicitly accept as a risk.
evidence: 'The MVP deliberately has no writer-only configuration switch or intermediate binary mode; attribution defects are recovered by pausing the affected creation ingress at the deployment boundary and rolling forward.'
No AC tests a partial-rollout or flag-gated scenario.
The plan's own Risks and Mitigations section acknowledges rollback is a 'full downgrade to the immediately pre-feature binary' — an expensive, all-or-nothing operation.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly rejects a feature flag or staged rollout ('The MVP deliberately has no writer-only configuration switch or intermediate binary mode'), meaning the behavior change reaches production as a 100%-traffic flip with no canary path.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — 'The public change is additive. The MVP deliberately has no writer-only configuration switch or intermediate binary mode'
scenarios: If a CREATE-path regression is discovered in production after deploy, operators have no flag to disable channel recording on new writes — they must either roll forward with a fix or execute a full binary downgrade with compaction pause.
In environments where rebar runs as a shared service across multiple teams, a 100%-traffic flip means all users are affected simultaneously with no gradual exposure.
findings.suggested_fix: Because the change is purely additive on CREATE writes (old readers ignore the field), the risk of a 100%-flip is lower than for a destructive change. If the team accepts this, add an explicit 'Risk Accepted' statement in Risks and Mitigations explaining why the additive contract makes staged rollout unnecessary, so reviewers can distinguish deliberate choice from oversight.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Architecture Decisions that writer-side enum validation applies to create_core callers (CLI/MCP/Python/import) but the Jira inbound path sets the channel value directly in CREATE data and relies on a reader-side fallback or a separate assertion; alternatively, add a lightweight validation call (e.g. assert channel in VALID_CHANNELS) inside _inbound_create_write_create_event to close the gap.
evidence: Plan: 'The shared core carries and validates the declared internal value; it does not inspect the call stack.'
Codebase: _inbound_create_write_create_event builds create_data and calls _write_event_file directly (apply_inbound_records.py lines 125–145), never routing through composer.create_core.

The plan itself acknowledges the two seams: 'The current code has two creation seams. Local surfaces converge on src/rebar/_commands/composer.py:create_core … while Jira inbound creation composes CREATE data in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py.'
ABSENCE: No plan text describes a validation step for the channel value on the Jira inbound path.
findings.finding: The plan states 'The shared core carries and validates the declared internal value' (Architecture Decisions), implying CREATE-time enum validation applies to all channels including 'jira'. However, the Jira inbound creation path in apply_inbound_records.py calls _write_event_file directly, bypassing create_core entirely — so the channel value written to Jira-originated CREATE events never passes through the proposed validator in create_core. If a future maintainer adds a new Jira inbound writer or mistypes the literal 'jira', no writer-side guard catches it.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions — 'The shared core carries and validates the declared internal value'; also src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py:_inbound_create_write_create_event (line 125, create_data dict)
scenarios: A future inbound Jira writer uses a typo or an off-vocabulary string; the error is silent at write time and surfaces only as an unexpected value in compiled state.
The plan's stated invariant that new writes always record a concrete non-unknown value cannot be enforced by create_core for the jira channel, only by convention.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Architecture Decisions: 'The inbound Jira materialization path sets creation_channel directly in CREATE data and does not route through create_core; a module-level assertion or constant reference in apply_inbound_records.py pins the vocabulary for that path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Architecture Decisions section that the creation_channel enum is canonically defined in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` (following the existing ticket_status/ticket_type pattern) and $ref'd from ticket_state.schema.json, export.schema.json, and any other consumer schema.
evidence: Parent plan Architecture section names ticket_state.schema.json only for the {"const": true} rule: 'ticket_state.schema.json encodes that rule formally as {"const": true}'
Parent plan never mentions common.schema.json as the enum's home
Child ticket 6fe2 What section: 'Add the canonical creation-channel enum to src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json with exactly cli, mcp, python, jira, import, unknown'

Existing pattern: ticket_status, ticket_type, relation, priority are all defined in common.schema.json and $ref'd from ticket_state.schema.json — the enum must follow this pattern but the parent plan does not state it
findings.finding: The parent plan's Architecture section specifies that the creation_channel enum vocabulary lives in ticket_state.schema.json (referencing the {"const": true} inference-marker encoding rule there), but never names common.schema.json as the canonical home of the enum definition itself. Child ticket 6fe2 correctly identifies common.schema.json as the canonical enum location, but an implementer reading only the parent plan cannot determine the authoritative schema file for the enum — creating a risk that it is added directly to ticket_state.schema.json (duplicating rather than $ref-ing) or to a new ad-hoc definition.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions section, first bullet: 'Add creation_channel to CREATE data and compiled ticket state with the closed compact vocabulary cli, mcp, python, jira, import, unknown.'
scenarios: An implementer working from the parent plan only adds the enum inline to ticket_state.schema.json instead of common.schema.json, causing the enum to be absent from export.schema.json and fetch_ticket_output.schema.json which both $ref ticket_state.schema.json without inlining its properties.
A future schema consumer generating types from common.schema.json misses the enum because it was never added there.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Architecture Decisions first bullet, append: 'The enum is defined once in common.schema.json as a $defs entry (following the ticket_status / ticket_type pattern) and $ref-d from ticket_state.schema.json and any output schema that exposes TicketState.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the ticket_state.schema.json (and common.schema.json) descriptions for `creation_channel` and `creation_channel_inferred` must include: the enum values, the absent-means-unknown legacy fallback, the inference predicate summary (three-signal Jira-only), and the trust boundary note ('diagnostic metadata, not a security attestation').
evidence: Architecture section specifies: 'infer jira only when all creation-stable signals agree: ticket_id starts with "jira-", author equals LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR, and env_id equals LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID'
ticket_state.schema.json currently has no creation_channel or creation_channel_inferred fields at all (confirmed by file read)
The plan instructs adding optional fields to ticket_state.schema.json but does not specify that the schema descriptions for those fields should reference or summarize the inference rule
Existing schema precedent: source_id description includes 'Present only on tickets created by rebar import' — a consumer-facing semantic note directly in the schema
findings.finding: The inference decision rule (_project_legacy_creation_channel's three-signal predicate) is specified in the Architecture section prose, but the ticket_state.schema.json schema definition for creation_channel and creation_channel_inferred — where an executor would look for field-level semantics — contains no reference to the inference rule, the predicate, or the trust boundary. The plan relies entirely on cross-section prose co-location rather than schema-adjacent specification.
findings.location: Architecture Decisions section, third bullet: inference predicate description vs. ticket_state.schema.json field definitions
scenarios: A downstream consumer reads the schema description for creation_channel_inferred and sees only the {"const": true} structural rule, with no indication of what inference criteria produced it or that it is absent on recorded values — leading to incorrect consumer logic treating absence as 'not inferred yet' rather than 'recorded or unknown'.
A future implementer modifying the inference logic does not know the schema description needs updating because the schema contains no mention of the predicate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in 6fe2's What/Approach the mechanism by which _mcp_writes.create_idea and log_session inject 'mcp' into the _lib_writes.idea() / _lib_writes.append_session_log() call chain (e.g. an _internal_channel kwarg on those wrappers, or MCP-specific entry points that bypass the public wrappers).
evidence: 6fe2 What: 'MCP create_ticket, create_idea, and log_session pass mcp'
_mcp_writes.py: create_idea calls rebar.idea(...) — routes through _lib_writes.idea() → composer.create_core
_mcp_writes.py: log_session calls rebar.append_session_log(...) — routes through _lib_writes.append_session_log → session_log.append → create_core
_lib_writes.py is listed in 6fe2 file_impact as 'Python defaults and internal MCP override plumbing'

The phrase 'internal MCP override plumbing' in the file_impact reason implies something is planned but the mechanism (a thread-through kwarg, a separate MCP entry point, a context variable) is not specified anywhere in 6fe2's What or Approach
container pairing: parent + 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/e622-a596-3ceb-4f74
findings.finding: MCP create_idea and log_session attribution path is underspecified: _mcp_writes.py calls rebar.idea() and rebar.append_session_log() which route through _lib_writes.py, but neither 6fe2's 'What' nor its file list specifies how 'mcp' overrides the 'python' default that _lib_writes.idea() and _lib_writes.append_session_log() would supply — creating a partial-without-remainder coverage gap where MCP's non-create_ticket paths may silently attribute as python.
findings.location: child 6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41 (What: 'MCP create_ticket, create_idea, and log_session pass mcp'; Files: _mcp_writes.py, _lib_writes.py)
scenarios: 6fe2 adds creation_channel='python' default to _lib_writes.idea() without adding an override mechanism; _mcp_writes.create_idea calls rebar.idea() with no channel override; MCP create_idea writes creation_channel=python — parent AC1 (mcp attribution for create_idea) fails.

6fe2 adds a context variable or internal kwarg but it is not visible in the public API and not tested — the test suite for MCP passes if only create_ticket is tested but create_idea/log_session are not separately k-filtered.
findings.suggested_fix: In 6fe2: add an internal creation_channel kwarg to _lib_writes.idea(), _lib_writes.append_session_log(), and _lib_writes.start_session_log() (default 'python'); _mcp_writes.py passes creation_channel='mcp' through these. The existing test k-filter 'mcp' in test_creation_channel.py should verify all three MCP creation tools (create_ticket, create_idea, log_session) independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in e622's Dependencies or What section that _version.py already carries the channel vocabulary constant added by 6fe2, and that e622's additions are additive to that base — ensuring the rebase sequence is explicit for reviewers.
evidence: 6fe2 file_impact: 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py: canonical runtime channel vocabulary'
e622 file_impact: 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py: define shared exact legacy Jira author and environment constants'
Both modify the same file; no explicit merge/rebase note in either story
e622 depends_on 6fe2 — sequential, so 6fe2 lands first; e622 must rebase onto 6fe2's _version.py
container pairing: parent + 568c-b44b-7e2b-4d87/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/e622-a596-3ceb-4f74
findings.finding: Both 6fe2 and e622 modify the same source file src/rebar/reducer/_version.py with different additions (6fe2: channel vocabulary enum; e622: LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR and LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID constants), but neither child's file_impact or What section acknowledges the shared write or specifies merge coordination — the sequential dep chain mitigates but neither story's scope or file ownership is explicit about this.
findings.location: child 6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41 (Files: reducer/_version.py) and child e622-a596-3ceb-4f74 (Files: reducer/_version.py)
scenarios: If e622 is developed against main before 6fe2 lands, e622's _version.py diff will conflict with 6fe2's additions at merge time — caught by Git but not anticipated in the specs.
A reviewer approving e622 in isolation sees only e622's _version.py additions and cannot verify the enum defined by 6fe2 is present.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to e622's Dependencies: '_version.py already defines the channel enum (6fe2); this story adds only LEGACY_JIRA_AUTHOR and LEGACY_JIRA_ENV_ID to the same file — rebase onto 6fe2 before merging.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
